title: Under 5 minutes to a deployed LLM endpoint — Audry Hsu, RunPod
summary: RunPod is a serverless LLM deployment platform enabling users to deploy LLM endpoints in under five minutes on H100 GPUs. The platform started in 2022 from repurposed crypto mining hardware and has grown to 500,000 developers with $120 million in annual recurring revenue, charging only for active request handling time.
writeup: A conference demo by a RunPod developer advocate covering the company's GPU cloud platform, with a live walk-through of deploying a vLLM serverless endpoint from the RunPod Hub in under five minutes. The talk spans the founding story (two crypto-mining rigs in a basement in 2022, a Reddit post offering free GPU access, bootstrapped to $120M ARR), a product overview across Pods, Serverless, Clusters, and Hub, and a hands-on deploy showing cold-start and execution latency. The through-line is that GPU infrastructure complexity should be abstracted away so developers can focus on building applications.
key_points: RunPod reached 500,000 developers, 30-plus data centers worldwide, and $120 million in annual recurring revenue, bootstrapped from a 2022 Reddit post by two founders whose crypto mining rigs sat idle after the market crashed.

The Serverless product auto-scales workers for bursty or batch workloads and charges only while a worker is actively handling a request, with configurable spending caps, max worker counts (up to 15 in the demo), and always-on workers that avoid cold-start latency for latency-sensitive paths.

Deploying a vLLM endpoint from the Hub requires no Dockerfile authoring: the Hub provides pre-vetted, pre-configured GitHub repos, and the console passes environment variables directly as vLLM serve flags, including context window length and max LoRA count.

In the live demo the first request sat in queue for 41 seconds due to cold start (model download from Hugging Face plus container initialization), but subsequent execution time was roughly 1.5 seconds, illustrating the tradeoff between always-on worker cost and first-request latency.

The endpoint exposes a standard HTTP API with built-in observability: per-endpoint telemetry shows request count, execution time, and queue delay time in the console without additional instrumentation.
